title: Chapter 35: Signing Back In
content: I wake up counting.

It's muscle memory at this point — the first thing I do every morning, before I open my eyes, before I check the light, before I remember who and what and where I am. I count. Because the overwrite always comes at 90 seconds. Every morning since the deductions started, I get ninety seconds of me, and then the contract reinitializes and the flatline drops back over everything like a curtain falling on an empty stage.
One. Two. Three.

I lie still in the dark and I count. The sheets are tangled around my legs and the apartment is quiet — no hum from Leo's servers, no keystrokes, just the ambient white noise of a city that never stops bleeding light through your windows. The morning is gray and thin and early. I don't know what time it is. I don't care. I'm counting.
Thirty. Thirty-one. Thirty-two.

My heart beats and I count. My lungs fill and empty and I count. This is the ritual. This is the only ritual I have left — ninety seconds of being a person before the contract reminds me I'm a subscription.
Fifty. Fifty-five. Sixty.
The number ticks past the halfway mark and my chest is still quiet. Still flat. Still the same hollow drum I've been living in. I keep counting. Of course I keep counting. This is what I do.
Seventy. Seventy-five. Eighty.

My fingers curl into the sheet. Any second now. The overwrite will drop and the window will close and I'll be the same Chloe I've been for eight weeks — functional, hollow, a body walking around with nobody home.
Eighty-five. Eighty-seven. Eighty-eight.
I'm holding my breath. I don't mean to. My lungs are burning and I'm holding my breath because something is different and I can't name it yet, I can't —
Eighty-nine.
Nine —
The overwrite doesn't come.
The window stays open.
Ninety-one. Ninety-two. Ninety-three.
I'm past the threshold and the curtain isn't falling and I'm still HERE, whatever here means, whatever I mean, and then it happens.
Warmth.

Not a ghost pulse. Not the phantom echo of an emotion that should be there and isn't. Not the gap where something used to live. Actual, physical warmth — a heat that starts somewhere behind my sternum and radiates outward like a pilot light catching in a room that's been dark for months. Like the first candle lit in a cathedral. Small and impossible and real.

I gasp. The sound escapes before I can stop it, a sharp intake of breath that tastes like morning and stale air and something else — something copper-sweet at the back of my throat that I don't recognize because I haven't tasted it in weeks.
Ninety-four. Ninety-five.

The warmth is spreading. My fingers. My sternum. The base of my skull. And with the warmth comes a sensation I know — I KNOW this sensation, I've been chasing its ghost for eight weeks — but it's not a ghost anymore, it's real, it's HERE, and it hits me like a wall.
Empathy.

I feel it arrive like an organ being placed back in my chest, settling into a space that's been vacant and aching and pretending it was fine. And suddenly — viscerally, overwhelmingly, without warning or permission — I feel what it was like for Leo to sit up all night writing code for someone who could not love him back. I feel the weight of his sacrifice, the impossible mathematics of choosing to give everything to someone who had nothing to give. I feel the 3 AM coffee and the shaking hands and the moment he hit Enter and sent the unDRM into the world, knowing it might work and knowing what it might cost him, and I feel him choosing me anyway, choosing all of us anyway, and the weight of that choice is so enormous that I can't breathe under it.

And it's not just Leo. I feel them — the 847,000. I feel them waking up in their apartments and warehouses and offices and beds, and the confusion that must be hitting them right now, the terror of feeling things they haven't felt in months or years, the relief that is also pain because relief hurts when you've been holding your breath that long. I feel a woman in a studio apartment in Queens gasping the same way I'm gasping. I feel a man in a shelter in Newark sitting up in his cot with tears streaming down his face and no idea why. I feel all of them and it's too much, it's TOO MUCH, I've been empty for so long and the flood is drowning me and I can't —

I curl into a ball on the bed. My knees hit my chest and my hands grab my own arms and I sob. Not performative crying, not the mechanical tear-production my body has been capable of on autopilot. SOBBING. The kind that comes from somewhere deeper than the lungs, somewhere spiritual and animal and ancient, somewhere the contract couldn't reach because it was never a line item, it was never a deductible emotion — it was just the human capacity to BREAK OPEN.
I sob and I sob and I sob, and it's the most alive I've felt in eight weeks.
The second wave comes while I'm still drowning in the first.
Trust.

It arrives not like a hit but like a hand — reaching down into the water and finding mine. I remember Leo's voice in the dark, quiet and certain: "I'll remember for both of us." God. GOD. He said that. He said that and he MEANT it, and I couldn't trust him when he said it because the contract had my trust in a glass case with a price tag, but now the glass is shattered and the trust is FREE and I feel it, I feel the truth of what he said, the absolute bone-deep reliability of someone who looked at a person who could not feel and chose to feel enough for two.

And Evelyn. Evelyn's hand in the courtroom, squeezing mine — when was that? Yesterday? A lifetime ago? — and I couldn't trust the gesture then, couldn't read it as anything more than a contractual obligation between a mother and a daughter bound by blood and damage. But now I feel it. I feel her hand and I feel the intention behind it, and the intention was LOVE, it was always love, love distorted by fear and pride and twenty years of bad decisions, but love all the same.
And then — like a key turning in a lock I didn't know was still locked — I hear her.
My grandmother's voice.

Not as data. Not as a transcript, not as a string of phonemes with metadata attached, not as the degraded audio file I've been clinging to like a prayer I couldn't believe in. As a SOUND. Warm and sweet and real, the way I used to hear it when I was small and the world was big and she was the biggest thing in it — "Chlooooeee." The way she said it, the elongated vowels like she was tasting the name, like the name itself was something delicious she wanted to savor. I can HEAR it. The voice is BACK.

I lunge for my phone on the nightstand. My hands are shaking so badly I drop it twice before I can unlock the screen. I find the voicemail — the one that was degrading, the one I played eight hundred times until the artifacts ate more of her than the Alzheimer's ever did — and I press play.

Her voice fills the room. Imperfect. Staticky. The compression artifacts are still there, the digital noise floor still hums underneath. But it's PRESENT. It's her. It's warm. It's the most beautiful sound I have ever heard in my entire life, and I have heard it Before, and I couldn't feel it then, and I can feel it NOW, and I am pressing the phone against my ear like I can push it through my skull into the place where she used to live.
I call Leo.
It rings.
It rings.
It rings.

His phone is dead. The breach killed it last night, or the battery finally gave out, or the universe is just like that sometimes — he's ALWAYS there and the one moment I need him most, the line is silent. I hang up. I dial the only other number that matters.
It rings twice.
"Chloe? It's 5 AM." Evelyn's voice is thick with sleep and suspicion and the guarded pre-caffeine tone she uses for everything before 7.
"My grandmother's voice is back."
Silence.
Not the empty silence of the flatline. Not the dead air of a connection that's failed. A LIVING silence. A silence that's DOING something. A silence so full of feeling that it can't fit into words.

Then Evelyn's voice comes back, and it's different from any way I've ever heard it — not Lawyer Hart, not the Wall, not the woman who cross-examined her own trauma into submission. Something cracked open. Something that's been sealed since before I was born.
"Oh, sweetheart."

She's never called me that. Not once. Not in twenty years of birthdays and holidays and courtroom corridors. Sweetheart. The word lands in my chest like a stone dropped into still water, and the ripples spread outward and outward and outward.

I can't speak. I just hold the phone against my ear and let my mother's voice be the thing that fills the room, and she doesn't say anything else, and neither do I, and the silence between us is the most intimate conversation we've ever had.
"Mom," I whisper. Just that. Just the word. The word I stopped using when I was fourteen and angry and she was impossible and I decided that "Evelyn" was the only name she deserved.
"I know," she says. "I know, baby."
And then I have to hang up because the third wave is here.
Grief.

It doesn't arrive. It COLLIDES. It hits like a truck doing eighty on a highway where the speed limit is hope, and I am the guardrail, and there is no guardrail. My grandmother is DEAD. She's been dead for two years. Two years, four months, and I don't know how many days because I stopped counting when the grief that was supposed to be counting got deducted from my account.

Now it's back. All of it. Compressed. Two years of unshed sorrow arriving at once like a tidal wave that's been held behind a dam that just shattered. I don't curl into a ball this time. I collapse. Off the bed, onto the floor, and the floor is cold and hard and REAL and I am crying in a way I didn't know human bodies could cry — not tears, RIVERS, not sobs, EARTHQUAKES, not grief, an ENTIRE OCEAN of grief that has been waiting patiently in the space where it was supposed to be.

I cry for my grandmother. For her voice and her hands and the way she called my name like it was a song she wrote. I cry for the sunset I watched from the Brooklyn Bridge three weeks ago — crimson and gold and lavender bleeding into each other like the sky was a painting of itself — and I couldn't FEEL it. I stood there and I saw it and I felt NOTHING. The most beautiful sunset in the history of sunsets and I was a tourist in my own awe, a ghost haunting the body of someone who used to know how to look up and be moved.

I cry for the person I was before the contract. The girl who drew in sketchbooks — bad drawings, terrible drawings, drawings that looked like a seagull having a seizure, but she drew them because drawing felt like FLYING and she didn't care if they were good. The girl who called her grandmother to describe sunsets in such excruciating detail that Gram would laugh and say, "Baby, just send me a picture," and she'd say, "No, you have to HEAR it," because she understood, even then, that some beauty only exists in the telling.

I cry for the hollowed version of myself who sat in federal court and testified about emotional theft without being able to feel why she was fighting. The version who said "they took my grief" in a voice as flat as a dial tone. The version who was RIGHT and couldn't FEEL right, who was fighting for her soul with a soul that was running on empty.
The grief is enormous. It is suffocating. It is the worst thing I have ever experienced.
It is beautiful.

Because the grief GIVES BACK what it takes. Every tear is proof that something was worth crying over. Every sob is evidence that I was someone worth sobbing for. The grief doesn't make me smaller — it makes me HUMAN. It fills the exact shape of the hollow, and the fit is terrible and perfect and I would not trade this agony for every numb morning in the world because this agony is MINE.
I am human. I am human. I am human.
The fourth wave comes more gently.

I'm on the kitchen floor — I always end up on the kitchen floor, it's like a law of physics, Chloe Hart + emotional catastrophe = kitchen floor — and the morning light is coming through the window now, real light, amber and soft and spilling across the linoleum like honey. I look around the apartment and I feel it: nostalgia.

Leo's dead monitors on the table, their screens dark and final. The cold coffee in the mug that says "BAD BITCHES DON'T NEED SLEEP" — a gift from Priya that I used to find hilarious and now find achingly, brutally sincere. The note-covered walls, my own handwriting looping and desperate, a paper trail of a woman trying to remember what it felt like to feel.

And I feel the ache of time passing. Not just the eight weeks of the flatline, but all the time — the years with my grandmother, the years without her, the years of Evelyn being a wall and me being a door that wouldn't knock. I feel the sweetness of the Before, when I was a girl with a sketchbook and a phone that rang with "Chlooooeee" and a whole unmortgaged soul. I mourn that girl. I celebrate that girl. She was BRAVE in a way I forgot how to be — brave enough to feel things without knowing what they cost, brave enough to love without reading the terms first.

The nostalgia is bittersweet. And the bittersweetness — the fact that I can taste BOTH notes at once, the sweet and the bitter, the memory and the loss, the gratitude and the sorrow — it's the most beautiful sensation I've ever experienced. It's a chord in a minor key. It's a sunset you watch alone and love anyway. It's the ache that proves you were alive enough to hurt.

I sit with it. I let it wash over me like the morning light, like the amber honey on the linoleum, and I don't try to separate the sweet from the bitter because they were never meant to be separated. That's the whole point. That's what the contract tried to teach me was a lie. That feeling things — ALL the things, the good and the bad and the ones that are both at once — is not a liability. It's the whole damn point.
The last wave doesn't arrive like the others.

The others were waves — crashing, flooding, overwhelming. This one is a sunrise. Slow. Warm. Unmistakable. It starts at the edges and works its way in, and by the time I realize what it is, it's already everywhere.
Love.

I think of Leo. His hoodie — the gray one with the coffee stain on the cuff that he wears like armor. His 3 AM coding sessions, the screen-light making his face look like a cathedral window, all shadows and devotion. His selfie video: "She made me feel like I was worth something when I was nothing," and the way his voice cracked on "nothing" like the word itself was too heavy for one person to carry.

I think of Evelyn. "Not even Hell" — two words in a courtroom that hit harder than any closing argument because they were the first honest thing she'd said in twenty years. And "sweetheart" this morning, the word she'd been holding like a stone in her mouth since I was four, finally set free.

I think of my grandmother. Whose voice is in my ears again, warm and sweet and real, "Chlooooeee," a name that has never sounded like anyone else's voice saying it, a name that belongs to the version of me she loved into existence.
And the love floods in.

Not a ghost pulse. Not a phantom limb. Not the echo of an emotion I used to have or the shadow of an emotion I should feel. A real, living, breathing EMOTION. It fills every hollow space the contract carved out of me — every cavity, every crater, every empty room where something used to live and died or was evicted or was repossessed by a company that traded on the NASDAQ. The love doesn't REPLACE what was lost. It INHABITS what was emptied. It moves into the same spaces and makes them livable again, not by pretending the emptiness didn't happen but by ACKNOWLEDGING that emptiness is just a room waiting for the right person to walk in.
I am Chloe Hart. And I can love.
I can LOVE.

The realization hits me like the warmth hit — physical, whole-body, undeniable — and I grab my phone again. Leo. I need to hear his voice. I need to tell him — I don't know what. I don't have the words. I've never had the words for this. But I need HIM.

I dial. It rings. And rings. And rings. Still dead. Of course it's still dead. He burned his whole setup pushing the unDRM through the gateway, and his phone is collateral damage in a war he fought for me, for all of me, for every hollowed person in the city and the country and the world, and I can't reach him and I need to reach him and I can't —

I get up. I don't think about it. My legs are shaking and my face is a disaster and I'm wearing a T-shirt with a coffee stain older than some of my friendships, and I don't care. I cross the apartment in seven steps and I throw the door open and —
Leo.
He's standing in the hallway.

He came in person because his phone died. Of course he did. Of course Leo Kim, who has never once in his life chosen the easy path when the hard one had me at the end of it, walked here in the predawn dark with his dead laptop under his arm. He looks like he's been to war. His eyes are red-rimmed and his hair is doing that thing where it sticks up on one side like a question mark and his hoodie — the gray one, the one with the coffee stain — is wrinkled in a way that suggests he hasn't taken it off in at least two days.

He smells like soap and circuit boards and coffee and the particular warmth of him that I have been breathing in without knowing it for weeks — the scent of someone who was there for me when there was nothing in it for him.
He looks at me. I look at him.
And I feel everything.

Not one emotion. ALL of them. Every single feeling I couldn't access for eight weeks — the empathy that made me understand him, the trust that made me believe him, the grief that proved I was human, the nostalgia that proved I had a past worth missing, and the love, the love, the LOVE — they all crash into me at once, and the impact is so enormous and so total that there's only one word for what I'm feeling, and the word has been sitting in the center of my chest like a stone since the first time he stayed up late to code something just because I needed it, and now the stone is a sun, and the sun is rising, and the only word, the ONLY word, is love.
I don't say it.

I grab him. My arms close around him so hard and so fast that the dead laptop nearly gets crushed between us, and I don't care, and he doesn't care, and his free arm comes around my back and his hand lands between my shoulder blades and I cry into his shoulder, great heaving sobs that shake both of us, and he holds me and I hold him and neither of us speaks.
Some things don't need words.

The morning light is pouring through the open door now, real and amber and warm, and I can hear the city waking up behind it — a siren, a car horn, the distant bass of someone's music from three floors down. Somewhere in Queens, a woman is gasping. Somewhere in Newark, a man is sitting up in his cot. 847,000 people are feeling things this morning that they couldn't feel yesterday, and their hearts are breaking and mending and breaking again, because that's what hearts do when they're WORKING.

The unDRM worked. The emotions are back. All of them. The warmth in my chest isn't going anywhere. I can feel my grandmother's voice and my mother's "sweetheart" and Leo's heartbeat against my cheek, and the world is enormous and terrifying and beautiful, and I can feel ALL of it.
But Leo's Section 89 is still pending.

They saved 847,000 souls, and his is still on the line. The victory is real, the threat is real, and for the first time in eight weeks, Chloe Hart can feel both at once — the joy and the fear, the love and the dread, the sunrise and the shadow it casts.
Feeling both at once isn't a contradiction.
It's being alive.
